---
title: "Architecture"
---

> *Target audience: a developer who wants to understand why batchcorder is
> designed the way it is — the tradeoffs, the concurrency model, and why
> specific libraries were chosen.*

## The problem

The Apache Arrow ecosystem models data streams as `RecordBatchReader`:
a forward-only, single-use iterator. Once consumed, it cannot be replayed.
This is intentional — Arrow streams can be arbitrarily large, generated from
network sockets or lazy computations, so buffering everything in memory is not
always feasible.

The problem surfaces whenever a downstream consumer needs to scan the same
source more than once:

- **DuckDB self-joins**: DuckDB calls `__arrow_c_stream__()` once per scan of
  a table reference. A query with two references to the same reader results in
  an empty second scan.
- **Multi-epoch training**: an ML training loop needs to iterate the same
  dataset once per epoch, but the upstream source (a network file system, a
  remote blob store) should only be read once.
- **Multiple independent consumers**: two workers consuming the same stream at
  different rates cannot share a raw reader.

The naive fix — collect everything into a `pyarrow.Table` — defeats streaming
for datasets that do not fit in memory.

## Design goals

1. **Single upstream read**: consume the source exactly once.
2. **Concurrent independent readers**: multiple handles, fully thread-safe,
   each with their own position.
3. **Bounded memory footprint**: spill to disk when the in-memory budget is
   exhausted.
4. **Arrow-native**: implement `__arrow_c_stream__` and `__arrow_c_schema__`
   so downstream libraries see a standard Arrow producer.
5. **Lazy ingestion**: don't require reading the whole source upfront; readers
   pull batches on demand.

## Key dependencies

### Foyer — the cache engine

[Foyer](https://github.com/foyer-rs/foyer) is a Rust crate providing a
hybrid in-memory + on-disk cache with Least-Recently-Used (LRU) eviction.
It was chosen over alternatives because:

- It handles the memory-to-disk spill automatically.
- Cache entries are byte-level blobs — straightforward to store IPC-serialised
  Arrow buffers.
- It is thread-safe and async-ready, fitting the Tokio runtime used by Foyer's
  I/O layer.

### PyO3 — Rust/Python FFI

[PyO3](https://pyo3.rs/) is the standard Rust crate for writing Python
extension modules. It handles GIL management, Python reference counting, and
the `#[pyclass]` / `#[pymethods]` macros that expose Rust structs to Python.

### Tokio — async runtime

Foyer's disk I/O is asynchronous. batchcorder embeds a Tokio runtime
(`tokio::runtime::Runtime`) and uses `runtime.block_on()` to run async cache
operations synchronously from Python's synchronous context.

## Core data structures

```
CachedDataset (Python-visible)
├── Arc<Inner>
│   ├── Mutex<UpstreamState>
│   │   ├── Option<ArrowArrayStreamReader>  ← upstream reader
│   │   └── ingested_count: usize
│   ├── HybridCache<u64, Bytes>             ← Foyer cache, keyed by batch index
│   └── Schema                             ← Arrow schema (from upstream)
└── ... (Python GC bookkeeping via PyO3)

CachedDatasetReader (Python-visible)
├── Arc<Inner>           ← shared reference to the same dataset
├── next_index: u64      ← this reader's current position
└── closed: bool
```

`Arc<Inner>` is a reference-counted pointer to the shared state. Multiple
`CachedDatasetReader` instances and the parent `CachedDataset` all hold a
clone of the same `Arc`, so the cache outlives any individual handle.

## Ingestion lifecycle

When a reader requests batch `N`:

1. If batch `N` is in the Foyer cache → deserialise from bytes and return it.
2. If not in the cache:
   a. Lock `UpstreamState`.
   b. While `ingested_count < N + 1`, pull the next batch from the upstream
      reader, serialise it to IPC bytes, and insert it into the Foyer cache
      with key `ingested_count`, then increment `ingested_count`.
   c. Release the lock.
   d. Fetch batch `N` from the cache (it must be there now).

This design means:

- The upstream reader is consumed **at most once**, in order.
- Multiple readers requesting the same batch concurrently will have one
  of them pull from upstream while the others wait on the lock, then all
  find the batch in the cache.
- The lock is only held while pulling from upstream — reading from the cache
  is lock-free from the application's perspective.

## Concurrency model and GIL safety

Python's GIL is held whenever PyO3 code runs in a Python thread. batchcorder
releases the GIL when doing I/O-bound work (Foyer disk reads/writes) by
using `py.allow_threads(|| { ... })` around cache operations.

Key invariants:

- The `Mutex<UpstreamState>` is a Rust `std::sync::Mutex`, not a Python lock.
  It is held only while pulling from upstream, not while reading from cache.
- `Arc<Inner>` clone/drop is atomic and does not touch the GIL.
- `CachedDatasetReader.next_index` is per-reader state accessed only from the
  owning reader — no synchronisation needed.

This means two threads can call `next()` on different readers simultaneously
without deadlocking, even if both readers happen to need the same uncached
batch (one will wait on the Rust mutex; the other will proceed after the
mutex holder populates the cache).

See [GIL and deadlock risks](gil-deadlock.md) for a deeper discussion.

## The PyCapsule Interface

`CachedDataset` and `CachedDatasetReader` implement
`__arrow_c_stream__` and `__arrow_c_schema__`. This means any Arrow-compatible
Python library — PyArrow, DuckDB, DataFusion, Polars — can consume them
without any explicit conversion:

```python
import pyarrow as pa
import duckdb

pa.table(ds)          # PyArrow reads via __arrow_c_stream__
duckdb.table("ds")    # DuckDB reads via __arrow_c_stream__
```

Each call to `__arrow_c_stream__` creates a fresh `CachedDatasetReader`
starting at batch 0, so every consumer sees a complete stream.

## Eviction and the "reader lag" invariant

Foyer evicts cache entries under memory/disk pressure using LRU. The
invariant batchcorder relies on is:

> *A cache entry for batch `N` must not be evicted before the slowest live
> reader has consumed batch `N`.*

batchcorder cannot enforce this invariant automatically — it depends on the
user sizing the cache to cover the maximum reader lag. If the invariant is
violated, the affected reader raises an `IOError` with a clear message
identifying the evicted batch index.
